task_completion.description: Create two simulated device adapters of different types in the OpenICE Supervisor.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The OpenICE Supervisor GUI is visible and shows at least two simulated medical device adapters of different types (e.g., simulated ECG, simulated pulse oximeter) actively running.

task_completion.description: Measure the OpenICE Java process's metrics using Linux command-line tools.
task_completion.visual_evidence: A terminal window is visible showing the execution of diagnostic commands (such as top, ps, jstat, jmap, or reading /proc) specifically targeting the OpenICE Java process to gather CPU, memory, and thread data.

task_completion.description: Create the performance report at the specified path.
task_completion.visual_evidence: A text editor (e.g., gedit, nano, vim) or terminal command shows the file being created or edited exactly at '/home/ga/Desktop/performance_baseline.txt'.

task_completion.description: Document the device types and exact commands used in the report.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The contents of the performance_baseline.txt file explicitly list the names of the two simulated device types created and the exact Linux/Java command-line tools used to collect the data.

task_completion.description: Document the specific numeric values for memory, CPU, and thread count with units.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The contents of the performance_baseline.txt file include specific numeric values with appropriate units for heap memory usage (MB), CPU usage (%), and thread count.

task_completion.description: Include a brief hardware sizing recommendation based on the metrics.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The contents of the performance_baseline.txt file include a concluding sentence or section providing a hardware sizing recommendation derived from the recorded metrics.

integrity.description: The agent interacted with the actual OpenICE application to create devices.
integrity.visual_evidence: Screenshots must show the OpenICE Supervisor GUI being manipulated (e.g., clicking to add simulated devices), rather than the agent just writing a fake report without launching the application.

integrity.description: The agent actually executed commands to measure the metrics.
integrity.visual_evidence: Screenshots must show terminal outputs with real, dynamic system data for the Java process, proving the agent didn't just invent the numbers for the report.

integrity.description: The values in the report match or are plausibly derived from the terminal measurements.
integrity.visual_evidence: The numeric values for CPU, memory, and threads written in the text file should reasonably correspond to the outputs visible in the terminal measurement screenshots.

privileged_info_for_vlm: OpenICE is a real medical device integration platform. The task requires creating exactly 2 simulated medical device adapters of DIFFERENT types in the OpenICE Supervisor GUI. The agent must use Linux/Java command-line tools (e.g., top, ps, jstat, jmap, /proc) to measure the OpenICE Java process. The final report must be located at /home/ga/Desktop/performance_baseline.txt and contain: 1) device types, 2) exact commands used, 3) numeric values for memory (MB), CPU (%), and thread count, and 4) a hardware sizing recommendation.
